title: Resources
text:
The previous style guide
This site replaces the older NCDPI Data Visualization Style Guide that lived as a Tableau workbook. The content here is being refreshed and expanded as we migrate it.

title: Resources
text:
Help

Branding questions: branding@dpi.nc.gov
Design help: graphics@dpi.nc.gov
This guide is published from GitHub.

title: Typography
text:
NCDPI charts, dashboards, and official web materials use Arial (fallback Helvetica) — the agency’s WCAG-compliant workhorse font. This guide deliberately sets its own body text in Arial too, so the words on the page match the words in the charts.

The NC Digital Commons web framework specifies Source Sans Pro for sites that live on nc.gov. This guide is an NCDPI-owned site about the chart palette, so it follows the NCDPI agency standard (Arial) for visual consistency with the charts.

title: Typography
text:
NCDPI charts, dashboards, and official web materials use Arial (fallback Helvetica) — the agency’s WCAG-compliant workhorse font. This guide deliberately sets its own body text in Arial too, so the words on the page match the words in the charts.

The NC Digital Commons web framework specifies Source Sans Pro for sites that live on nc.gov. This guide is an NCDPI-owned site about the chart palette, so it follows the NCDPI agency standard (Arial) for visual consistency with the charts.

title: Typography
text:
Text sizes in charts
These come straight from the brand color-and-type CSV that drives the whole guide:



Role
Size

Chart title

Subtitle / note

title: Typography
text:
Rules

Body and paragraph text is black or DPI Blue.
Don’t color-code text for emphasis — use bold instead.
Section titles and headings on this site are DPI Blue. (In charts, the title is black and the dashboard title is DPI Blue — see the table above.)
If everything stands out, nothing stands out.

title: Color — sequences
text:
Use a sequential palette when the data has a natural order from low to high. NCDPI provides three, because in education “high” isn’t always “good”:
Darker always means more — the dark-is-more convention people read intuitively.

title: Color — sequences
text:
Explore the palettes
Pick a direction and the number of steps:


The steps are equally spaced in color space. Each step is the same perceptual distance from the next, from the neutral end to the full color. So the values they stand for should be equally spaced too — even color steps imply even bins (0–20, 20–40, …), not arbitrary cutoffs.


Don’t let color do all the work. Adjacent steps are close in lightness and won’t all meet WCAG contrast against each other. Keep a labeled legend or value labels, and add a thin white border where segments touch (pie, treemap, stacked, map).


Continuous variants. Each sequence also has a smooth, stepless continuous ramp. Reach for it only where the data is truly continuous and position can’t carry the magnitude — almost always a choropleth map or a heatmap. For anything with countable categories, use the stepped palettes above.

title: Color — sequences
text:
Example
A sequential palette earns its keep where position can’t show magnitude — here a heatmap, where color carries the value. Match the palette to the meaning: a proficiency view uses Sequence to Good; shade a chronic-absenteeism view with Sequence to Bad.

title: Color — highlights
text: The single most useful move in the whole palette: plot everything in neutral grey and use color for the one thing your message is about. This is how you handle “too many categories” without breaking the five-color rule.

title: Color — highlights
text:
Example
Eight regions, all neutral grey except one in navy. The eye lands on the focal region immediately — and you’ve spent only one color to do it.

title: Color — diverging
text:
Use a diverging palette when the scale has a meaningful midpoint and runs in two directions from it — change versus last year, performance versus a target, or Likert agreement. Rust on one side, teal on the other, neutral grey in the middle.
NCDPI provides 3-, 5-, 7-, and 9-step versions so you can match the number of classes in your data. The steps read like a number line — negative through zero to positive (Step -2 … Step 0 … Step +2).

title: Color — diverging
text:
Explore the steps


Steps are symmetric and equally spaced around the midpoint. Match the data to that shape: a 5-step palette fits five ordered classes centered on a true middle (Strongly disagree … Neutral … Strongly agree), not lopsided cutoffs. If your data isn’t centered on a real zero/neutral, a sequence is the better fit.


Don’t let color do all the work. Steps near the midpoint are close in lightness and won’t all meet WCAG contrast against each other. Keep a labeled legend or value labels, and add a thin white border where segments touch.


The School Performance Grade colors (A → F) are the 5-step diverging palette in action: A is teal, C is neutral grey, F is rust. You’ll see it on the pie and map examples.

title: Color — diverging
text:
Example
Likert data is the textbook diverging case: a diverging stacked bar runs from disagree (rust) through neutral (grey) to agree (teal), centered on the neutral response.

title: Maps
text: Maps answer “where?” They’re powerful and familiar, but color-on-geography is one of the least precise encodings — always pair a map with a chart that allows precise comparison.

title: Maps
text:
Choropleth map
Shades each district by a rate (here, the share of students chronically absent). Good for “where is the rate highest?” — bad at communicating exact values, because position is fixed by geography and color is a low-precision encoding. Big rural districts also pull the eye more than small urban ones with the same value.




Choropleth vs. dot-density: which to use. A choropleth answers “where is the rate highest?” — values are normalised to area (percent, per-capita, average), so a small dense district and a large rural one are comparable. A dot-density map answers “where are the things?” — it shows counts in place, so density patterns are honest but rates aren’t. Pick the one that matches your question, or show both.

title: Maps
text:
Dot-density map
Plots each school by location. Honest about where the schools are, but doesn’t support reading any per-district rate from the map alone.




On any map, put a sorted bar or dot plot alongside it. The map shows the pattern; the bar lets people read the values.

title: Chart types
text:
Charts are organised here by the job they do — the message you want your audience to take away — not by the data type. Not sure which job you have? The Chart Chooser will walk you to a recommendation.
Every example uses real, public NCDPI school data and the NCDPI brand palette.


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
    
    Compare and rank
    Bar charts, dots, lollipops, dumbbells
  

  
    
      
        
        
          
          
          
          
          
          
        
      
    
    Change over time
    Lines, small multiples, slope charts
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
      
    
    Parts of a whole
    Pie, stacked bar, waffle
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
        
        
        
      
    
    Distribution
    Histogram, box plot
  

  
    
      
        
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
        
      
    
    Relationships
    Scatter, bubble
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
        
      
    
    Difference from a baseline
    Diverging bars, dumbbells, Likert
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
      
    
    Geographic patterns
    Choropleth, point, hex maps
  

  
    
      
        92%
      
    
    Headline numberComing soon
    BAN (Big Aggregate Number), KPI tile

title: Difference from a baseline
text: When the difference itself is the message — change from last year, above/below a target, or the gap between two groups — plot the difference directly.

title: Difference from a baseline
text:
Graph the delta
Don’t make the reader subtract two bars by eye. A diverging bar against a zero line puts the change front and centre, and a constant gap stays the same size everywhere (two raw lines make a fixed gap look like it shrinks as values rise).

title: Difference from a baseline
section: Dumbbell: the gap between two points
text:
Dumbbell: the gap between two points
A dumbbell keeps both values but draws attention to the distance between them.

title: Difference from a baseline
text:
Rating-scale (Likert) data
Centre a diverging stacked bar on the neutral response: disagree extends left, agree extends right. Never use a pie for survey data.




The survey numbers above are synthetic demo data, not real NCDPI results.

title: Bar charts
text: The bar chart is the workhorse of comparison: it encodes values as length on a common baseline, which is one of the most accurate things the human eye can do.

title: Bar charts
text:
Comparing categories
When you have one number per category, sort by value and use a single color. With more than five categories you do not need a different color for each bar — position already does the work.




Always start the value axis at zero. Bars encode magnitude by length, so a truncated axis exaggerates differences and misleads the reader.

title: Dashboard elements
text: A dashboard is a set of charts that share context. The chrome around them should be calm and predictable so the data stands out.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI dashboard



Anatomy of an NCDPI dashboard — title and subtitle, BAN row, supporting charts, filter rail, and footer chrome.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Titles and text

Dashboard title: DPI Blue, 26 pt. Subtitle: dark grey, 16 pt.
Help text (how to read a chart, how to use a filter): Plum #842175, 11 pt — the same treatment as chart annotations. Help text and annotations are both meta-overlays on data, so they share a color so users learn the convention once.
Body text is black or DPI Blue — never color-coded for emphasis.


Common dashboard chrome
Every NCDPI dashboard should carry, somewhere visible (usually the footer):

Data last updated — a date, plus the refresh cadence if known (“Updated 2025-09-15, monthly”).
Contact — a name or shared inbox for questions and corrections.
NCDPI logo — small, top-left or footer, so attribution is unambiguous.
Glossary or help link — for any term a non-expert reader might stumble on.

These four items add almost no visual weight but answer the questions every new viewer has within thirty seconds.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Controls and filters

Group filters together in a consistent place (top or right rail) so users learn where to look once.
Label the selected state clearly (darker) versus unselected (grey).
Default to the most useful view, not an empty one — don’t make users configure the dashboard before it says anything.


Keep the filter count low
Every filter is one more decision the user has to make before the dashboard answers anything (Hick’s Law — choice time grows with the number of options). Aim for the minimum set of filters that lets users answer the question the dashboard is built for. If you can’t answer “what does this filter unlock?” in one sentence, the filter probably shouldn’t be there.


When you can’t trim, use progressive disclosure
Sometimes a stakeholder mandates more filters than really fit. In that case, don’t put them all on the page at once — hide secondary filters behind a collapsible panel (Tableau: dynamic zone visibility) or a “More filters” toggle. Show the two or three most-used filters by default and let the user reveal the rest if they need them.


Prefer cross-filtering over filter controls
The most intuitive filter is the chart itself. Let the user click a bar on a top-left chart and watch the rest of the dashboard re-scope to that selection. Cross-filtering replaces chrome with direct manipulation — fewer dropdowns, more discovery.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Layout

Reading order: Z-pattern, top-left first
Western readers scan dashboards in a rough Z: top-left first, eye sweeps across to the top-right, drops down and across again to the bottom-right. The top-left position earns the most attention — put your most important number or chart there.


Drill down as you move down and right
Pair the Z-pattern with a progressive zoom. Top-left should be the 30,000-ft view — the headline number or trend. As the eye moves down and to the right, content gets progressively more granular: state → region → district → school. This matches Shneiderman’s mantra for information visualization: overview first, zoom and filter, details on demand.


Big Aggregate Numbers (BANs) at the top
The headline number for the whole dashboard usually deserves to be the very first thing the eye lands on. A row of BANs — single, large numerals with a short label — anchors the dashboard, sets the scale, and gives the rest of the charts a reference point. Use them sparingly (three to five at most) and label the time period clearly.

BAN page coming soon. BANs are not yet listed as a chart type on this site — a dedicated page is on the roadmap. In the meantime, keep them simple: one big number, one short label, one tiny period of comparison (“↑ 2.1 pts vs. 2022”).



Interactivity earns its keep
Hover for detail, click to highlight, filter to focus. In a dashboard you can show more than in a static chart because the user controls what to look at — but every interactive feature is one more thing to discover. Make the common path obvious.

Dashboards are where highlight-one-on-grey shines: plot every series in grey and let the user click to spotlight the one they care about — no color overload, any number of categories.

title: Chart Gallery
text: Every worked example from the Chart Chooser on one page, grouped by purpose. Use it to browse what an on-brand NCDPI version of each chart type looks like — or to find a type by eye when you’d rather scan than answer the chooser’s questions.

title: Chart Gallery
text:
Used elsewhere in the guide
Two more specs that aren’t chooser examples but appear on the color pages — included here so this page shows every live chart in the guide.

  Category-group bar
  charts/groups__category-bar.vl.json
  


  Spotlight (highlight) bar
  charts/highlight__spotlight-bar.vl.json

title: Small multiples
text: A small-multiples chart repeats the same little chart once per category, on a shared scale, in a grid. It is the most reliable cure for “I have too many categories” — and it’s badly under-used at NCDPI, so reach for it more.

title: Small multiples
text:
Why they work

No color overload. Each panel uses one color, so the five-color ceiling never binds, no matter how many categories you have.
Honest comparison. A shared scale means a tall bar in one panel really is taller than a short bar in another.
Pattern at a glance. The eye sweeps the grid and spots the outlier panel immediately.

title: Small multiples
text:
How to build them well

Keep every panel on the same scale — that’s the whole point.
Order the panels meaningfully (by value, region, time) rather than alphabetically.
One color across all panels; highlight a reference (the state average) the same way in each.
Stop around 20–30 panels — past that, each is too small to read.

title: Best practices
text: A handful of principles, drawn from our data visualization literature review, explain why the rules on this site are the rules. Read these once and most chart decisions answer themselves.

title: Best practices
section: NCDPI standing rules, in one place
text:
NCDPI standing rules, in one place


Five colors maximum (three or four is better).
Promote small multiples — they’re under-used here.
Stacked bars: 2–3 segments only. Beyond that, separate bars on a common baseline.
Break lines at missing years (e.g., the 2020–21 COVID gap) — never interpolate.
Graph the delta — when the message is a difference, plot the difference itself.

title: Annotation
text: A chart that makes the reader figure out the point often loses them. Do the interpreting for them.

title: Annotation
section: Write the takeaway into the title
text:
Write the takeaway into the title
A title like “Average SPG score by region” describes the chart. A title like “Five regions gained ground since 2022; the Sandhills lagged” tells the reader what to see. Lead with the message; let the axis labels handle the description.

title: Annotation
text:
Label directly, annotate the story

Direct-label lines and bars instead of relying on a legend (up to ~7 items).
Call out the one or two points that carry the argument — the outlier, the crossover, the target line — with a short text note in plum #842175, the brand annotation color.
Mark context the data needs: a policy change, a target, the COVID gap.

title: Annotation
text:
Less chart, more meaning
Annotation is not decoration. Each note should answer a question the reader would otherwise have to ask. If a viewer can look at the chart and immediately say what it means, the annotation has done its job.

This pairs with the perceptual hierarchy: pick the chart that encodes the data accurately, then annotate it so the meaning is as easy to read as the values.

title: Color rules
text: Color is the most abused encoding in agency charts. Three rules keep it honest.

title: Color rules
text:
1. Five colors is the ceiling
People can hold only a handful of categorical hues apart before search slows and errors climb; three to four is the comfortable range. When you have more categories, the answer is never more colors — it’s highlight-one-on-grey or small multiples.

title: Color rules
text:
2. Dark is more
On a sequential scale, people read darker as a larger value by default. Honour it: don’t make your lightest shade the biggest number. NCDPI’s sequential palettes are built dark-is-more already.

title: Color rules
text:
3. Color should mean something

Match the palette to the data: categorical for unordered groups, sequential for ordered, diverging for a meaningful midpoint.
Make “good” and “bad” directions deliberate — teal for good, rust for bad.
Never rely on color alone. Pair it with labels, position, or shape, so the chart still works for color-blind readers and in greyscale.


Run any color-on-color text combination through the WebAIM contrast checker. DPI Aqua at full strength is not accessible for small text — use Medium #077890 or Dark #006174 aqua instead.

title: The perceptual hierarchy
text: Some visual encodings are read more accurately than others. Cleveland & McGill’s classic experiments, replicated many times since, rank them roughly like this:

title: The perceptual hierarchy
text:
What it means in practice

To let people read precise values, encode with position or length — a dot plot or bar, not a heatmap.
Use color and area for the broad pattern (a map, a treemap), and pair them with a position/length chart when the exact numbers matter.
This is exactly why the Chart Chooser, when in doubt, prefers the chart higher on this list.


Pie charts sit mid-hierarchy on angle — which is why they’re fine for “which slice is biggest?” with a few slices, and poor for comparing many similar slices. The rule (“five slices, no more”) falls straight out of the science.

title: Chart Chooser
text:
Answer a few quick questions about your data and what you want to show. You’ll get one to three chart types that fit — each with a small, on-brand NCDPI example and tips for building it well. This follows the same evidence-based logic as our chart-selection guidance; it never recommends a chart that misleads.

  The Chart Chooser needs JavaScript. See the Chart types gallery for the same guidance.

title: Chart elements
text: The non-data parts of a chart should recede so the data can speak. NCDPI defaults keep them quiet and consistent.

title: Chart elements
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI chart
Every piece of the chart below is set by the brand spec — fonts, sizes, colors, and spacing — so a reader who has seen one NCDPI chart already knows how to read the next one.



Anatomy of an NCDPI chart — labeled callouts for title, subtitle, axis labels, axis values, data marks, annotation, gridlines, and source note.

title: Chart elements
text:
Gridlines, axes, and rulers

Gridlines are light grey #DEDEDE — present enough to support reading a value, faint enough to disappear behind the data.
Axis rulers and dividers use the same light grey. Drop the top and right spines entirely.
Reference lines (a target, the state average) are DPI Blue #003A70 so they read as deliberate, not as data.

title: Chart elements
text:
The zero-baseline rule
Bars and areas encode value by length or area, so they must start at zero. Truncating the axis exaggerates differences and is the single most common way a chart misleads. Lines and dot plots may use a non-zero baseline — but say so if you do.

title: Chart elements
text:
Labels over legends
When there are seven or fewer items, label them directly rather than making the reader bounce between a legend and the data. Reserve legends for when direct labels would collide.

Declutter, don’t decorate. Every gridline, tick, and label should earn its place. If removing it doesn’t cost the reader anything, remove it.

title: Table elements
text: Sometimes a table is the right answer — when readers need to look up exact values. Make it scannable.

title: Table elements
text:
Example

North Carolina public schools and student population by urbanicity, 2023–24


Urbanicity
Schools
Student pop.
% of NC student pop.




City
612
472,300
31.4%


Suburb
498
418,900
27.8%


Town
331
186,200
12.4%


Rural
1,074
427,500
28.4%



Total
2,515
1,504,900
100.0%





Source: NCDPI school directory, 2023–24. Counts exclude charters with statewide enrollment.

title: Table elements
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI table
Same idea as the chart anatomy — name each part, then its spec. A table simply has fewer parts. Match these and it reads as a clean look-up grid.



Part
Specification




Title (the <caption>)
14 pt, bold,  Black #000000 — sized to sit above the header row in the hierarchy


Header row
White text on  DPI Blue #003A70 — short, strong labels


Row header
Leftmost cell of each row; left-aligned, regular weight


Row banding
 10% DPI Blue #E6EDF6 on alternate rows — guides the eye across a row without shouting


Dividers
 Light grey #DEDEDE


Total row
2 px top rule, bold, no fill — separates the summary from the data


Source note
10 pt,  Dark grey #525A60 — a table's source note sits one step below the brand's 11 pt chart note, so it recedes

title: Table elements
text:
Alignment

Numbers align right and share decimal places, so magnitudes line up visually.
Text aligns left.
Headers match the alignment of the column beneath them.

title: Table elements
text:
Make tables do more

Sort by the column that matters, not alphabetically by default.
Add an embedded sparkline column to show a trend per row without a separate chart.
Lightly shade or mark the values that carry the message — don’t make readers hunt.

A few small in-cell techniques — one row each. They borrow the chart palette so the table still reads as NCDPI:

In-cell techniques (illustrative)







Technique
Example
Reach for it when…




Sparkline



each row has a short trend (say, attendance 2019–2024) and a full line chart would be overkill. Color the line navy #003A70; dot the last point.


In-cell data bar
86  



readers compare magnitudes down a column at a glance — the bar length does work the bare number can't. Keep the value visible alongside it.


Heat cell
12 47 93
spotting highs and lows across a grid. Shade with Sequence to Neutral (light grey → #5085BC), dark = higher. Mind contrast if you also print the value: dark ink on the darkest steps fails WCAG AA for small text (~4.2:1), and white text is worse — so keep the in-cell number only where the shade is light, or show the value outside the cell.




One enrichment per table, not all three at once. These are accents on a look-up grid — if every column has a bar or a heat tint, you’ve built a cluttered chart, not a table.

title: Table elements
text:
Accessibility

Most NCDPI tables are built in Tableau, PowerPoint, or Word — not hand-coded. The principles here apply in every tool: give the table a real title/caption, use genuine header rows (not bold text faking a header), keep one value per cell (avoid merged cells), and never use a table just for page layout. The HTML attributes shown below (scope, headers/id) are how you satisfy these when you hand-code a table — in Tableau and Office, use the built-in table, crosstab, and caption features and they handle most of it for you.

A data table is one of the few places where structure directly affects whether a screen-reader user can use the table at all — so the underlying markup (or your tool’s accessibility features) matters more here than it does for a chart.

Use <th scope="col"> on column headers (and scope="row" on the leading cell of each row where it makes sense). WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships requires that the table’s structure be programmatically conveyable — scope is how you do that.
Give every table a <caption> or aria-label. The caption answers “what is this table about?” before the screen reader starts reading cells.
Avoid merged header cells. WCAG doesn’t ban them outright, but screen-reader support for merged cells is uneven, and they make linear navigation confusing. If you truly need a merged header (a complex multi-level table), mark it up with explicit headers / id attributes so each data cell points to the headers that govern it.
Don’t use a table for layout. If the goal is page layout, use CSS — not <table>.


A simple, well-marked-up table beats a clever, complex one every time. If you find yourself reaching for merged cells, ask whether two simple tables would tell the same story better.

title: Table elements
section: Pair charts with an accessible table
text:
Pair charts with an accessible table
Even a perfect chart leaves screen-reader users out of the conversation. For any complex chart in a dashboard or slide deck, give them a way to reach the underlying numbers.

In Tableau: add a “View data” button that toggles a hidden cross-tab via dynamic zone visibility. Default-hidden so it doesn’t crowd the visual; one click for users who need it.
In PowerPoint: put the chart on one slide and add a “Data table” hyperlink to a companion slide that holds the corresponding cross-tab.
In a web report: offer a “Show table” toggle beneath the chart, or link to a downloadable CSV.


A table answers “what is the exact value?”; a chart answers “what is the pattern?” If your reader needs both — or if accessibility requires both — give them both.

title: Relationships
text: When you want to show whether two numbers move together, plot them against each other. Position on two scales is the most accurate read there is.

title: Relationships
text:
Scatterplot
Each dot is a school. The shape reveals direction, strength, and outliers. Add a trend line if the relationship is the headline.

title: Relationships
text:
Bubble chart
Add a third measure as bubble area — but read area as approximate, never precise, and keep the count modest.




Correlation isn’t causation, and a trend line invites that leap. Annotate what the relationship does and doesn’t mean for your audience.

title: Distributions
text: Reach for a distribution chart when the spread is the story — not a single average, but how values are arranged around it.

title: Distributions
text:
Histogram
Shows the shape of one numeric variable: where schools cluster, and whether the spread is symmetric, skewed, or has two humps.

title: Distributions
text:
Box plot
Compares several distributions at once — median, quartiles, and outliers side by side. For staff and analyst audiences; add a one-line explainer for the public.




A single average can hide a bimodal or skewed distribution. If two schools both “average a C” but one is all-C and the other is half-A/half-F, the distribution is the honest picture.

title: Lines & change over time
text: Use a line when the message is the trend — direction and rate of change across time. The connecting line emphasises continuity, so only use it for genuinely sequential (time) data.

title: Lines & change over time
text:
A few series
Keep it to about five colored lines. Direct-label the ends rather than making the reader bounce to a legend.




Break the line at missing years. NC issued no School Performance Grades in 2020 and 2021. A line drawn straight through that gap would invent two years of data. Show the break.

title: Lines & change over time
text:
Many series → small multiples
More than five lines becomes spaghetti. Give each category its own panel on a shared scale instead. Small multiples are under-used at NCDPI — reach for them.

title: Lines & change over time
text:
Two time points → slope chart
When you only have a “before” and “after,” a slope chart shows each item’s value at both points and the direction of change, and makes rank reversals pop.

title: Parts of a whole
text: Part-to-whole charts answer “what share does each piece make up?” They work best when there are only a few pieces.

title: Parts of a whole
text:
Pie chart
Fine with five slices or fewer, and genuinely good for “which is biggest?” Order slices largest-to-smallest and label them directly.




Five slices is the ceiling. Beyond that, angle judgements collapse — group the small categories into “Other,” or switch to a sorted bar chart.

title: Parts of a whole
text:
Stacked bars: keep them shallow
A 100% stacked bar shows composition across categories, but only the bottom segment sits on a common baseline. Keep stacked bars to 2–3 segments. With four or more, the middle segments float and can’t be compared — use separate bars on a common baseline (grouped bars or small multiples) instead.

title: Color — category groups
text:
Use the group palettes for distinct, unordered categories — regions, subjects, school types. The palettes are pre-built for two through six categories so you never have to guess. Stop at five; if you need more, see Highlights or use small multiples.
These swatches are generated live from the brand color file.
Two categories
Three categories
Four categories
Five categories (the practical maximum)
Six categories (use only when truly unavoidable)

title: Color — category groups
text:
Example
Four locales, four brand colors applied in order — with a white border so neighbours stay crisp. Position and labels do the identifying; color just groups.

title: Color lookup
text: Every color in the NCDPI chart system, searchable. Filter by palette, element, or hex code. This table is generated directly from the brand color file, so it never drifts out of date.

title: Color — overview
text:
The NCDPI chart palette is built for four jobs. Pick the palette that matches the kind of data, not your favorite color.
Throughout this guide we name colors by their HEX value (e.g., #5085BC), not RGB. Every value lives in the searchable Color lookup.

title: Color — overview
section: The one rule that matters most
text:
The one rule that matters most

Five distinct colors is the ceiling — three or four is better. Past five hues, people can’t reliably tell categories apart. When you have more, don’t add colors: highlight one against grey, or use small multiples (one small panel per category). Every extra hue is one more thing the reader has to decode — keep the count low so their attention goes to the differences that matter, not to working out which color means which category.

title: Color — overview
text:
Category order
When you do color-code categories, use the brand order so charts stay consistent — Blue → Orange → Purple → Green → Gold → Plum:


See Category groups for how many to use at each step. Never rely on color alone — pair it with labels or position.

title: Make every NCDPI chart clear, honest, and on-brand.
